body:
@koshibb 

Alternate and time-taking way,

To upgrade (without clean install) to Pro from Home, 
browse this registry,
`HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion`
and change the `EditionID` key value to `Professional`

Now run setup.exe from Windows 10/11 latest ISO and do an in-place upgrade. That's it.

You can download the latest ISO from https://tb.rg-adguard.net/public.php

body:
Some of the terms used here are incorrect but I do get your point that in some cases when another KMS activator is used and later MAS online KMS option is used then it gets stuck. 

The issue was not related to KMS host but added rules for SppExtComObj.exe and sppsvc.exe in IFEO registry entry. 

MAS 1.6 solves this issue. Please let me know if you can reproduce this error with MAS 1.6
author: WindowsAddict

body:
I have reproduced this problem several times on many PCs to make sure it was this exact same problem!

I had tried every possible fix mentioned in **Stack Overflow** and the **Microsoft Support Forums**,
from simple time/date sync to `Dism /RestoreHealth & SFC /ScanNow`, as well as every troubleshooting tip in your homepage.

Until I stumbled upon [this article](https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/topic/kms-error-0xc004f074-when-you-try-to-activate-office-2016-volume-licensed-client-89502352-f7eb-7dc2-559a-7aae7446efbf) from Microsoft, which described the cause for this error as: 

> The issue occurs when you cohost the Office 2013 KMS host and the Office 2016 KMS host on the same system, and that system is running either Windows 7 or Windows Server 2008 R2.

After reading this I thought that removing all KMS Hosts might fix the problem,
so I searched for the command I mentioned above, which indeed resolved this issue in every PC.

Every PC I tried this on, was running either **Windows 10** or **11**, not 7 as mentioned in the article
and running **Office 2016 only** (never had 2013- or 2019+), but they all used multiple KMS activators in the past.

That's why I came to the conclusion, that this was the real problem behind the scenes.

_**NOTE:** I referred to the wrong error code above, it was actually: **0xC004F074**._
_**NOTE 2:** When I have free time, I will try to reproduce this error and use MAS 1.6 to see if it can fix it._

body:
> https://github.com/massgravel/Microsoft-Activation-Scripts/issues/132#issue-1351482654

@Mukul1127, it's not generally a good idea to use aliases, though. They're fragile, since if anything in `$PATH` overrides them, you'll be calling another command, and they're frequently deprecated without much grace period. They're not even consistent across editions of PowerShell (like Core). Better to expand them to their commandlets.

body:
I am afraid you're a bit late to the party (This change was made years ago!).
We haven't seen any users report issues with the shortened aliases rather than using the expanded commandlets, so your concerns have ended up not being an issue, thank you for the feedback though
author: thecatontheceiling

body: > > [#132 (comment)](https://github.com/massgravel/Microsoft-Activation-Scripts/issues/132#issue-1351482654) > > [@Mukul1127](https://github.com/Mukul1127?rgh-link-date=2025-01-26T11%3A47%3A34.000Z), it's not generally a good idea to use aliases, though. They're fragile, since if anything in `$PATH` overrides them, you'll be calling another command, and they're frequently deprecated without much grace period. They're not even consistent across editions of PowerShell (like Core). Better to expand them to their commandlets. Hm, I didn't know that, maybe we could add a note to the docs using `iwr -useb`. I believe `irm` works in all versions of PowerShell though and I don't know of any tool using that name.

body:
@hooddy 

As discussed in discord server, it won't work because arm32 doesn't have x86 emulation. So our patching method for x86 gatherosstate.exe won't work. Also, as tested its arm32 gatherosstate failed to generate a ticket which means it won't work even if we make a patch for it. 

Besides, it's just a leaked build and no official build was ever released or will be released. It's just a lost cause. No point in investing efforts in it.

However, if you still need HWID activation, contact us on discord, we have some alternative methods to get activation, that may work.

body:
Licensing token file in folder `%WINDIR%\System32\spp\store\2.0` contains data for Windows and Office both. 
Theoretically, it's possible to clean only Office license files in licensing token, however, in many conditions, a rebuild of the token file from scratch is required.

Anyway, don't worry that you will lose your Windows license in this license rebuilding, system will auto-activate if the system is activated with HWID (digital license) or the license is stored in the motherboard.

author: tijon1

body:
Thanks for the reply. SBS uses it's own CAL and licensing system, but the activation looked normal. I don't think I can put in a server 2003 volume key. I tried the trick in http://karthi-techbits.blogspot.com/2010/11/windows-server-2003-standard-r2.html and so far it seems to work:

Navigate to the following registry key:
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\WPAEvents

5. Note: From this step onward, the cracking process must be performed as quickly as possible, as it hacks activation stuff and Windows may change back the setting if there is a delay of more than few seconds.

Locate a string value named OOBETimer.
6. Right-click on OOBETimer, and click on Modify.
7. Change the value to FF D5 71 D6 8B 6A 8D 6F D5 33 93 FD.

Activation OOBETimer
8. Click on OK.
9. Right-click on WPAEvents and select Permissions?.
10. Click on Advanced button.
11. Uncheck the check box for Inherit from parent the permission entries that apply to child objects. Include these with entries explicitly defined here.
12. Click on Apply.
13. When prompted, click COPY.
14. From there, highlight the SYSTEM line, and click Edit.
15. Choose the Deny option to ban all users from accessing and editing the key.
16. Click on OK.
17. Reboot the computer.

Maybe this could be put in "unsupported activations" in the web page at some point, or close the issue.

body:
I have fixed the issue, it was nothing to do with activation.
for some reason the cache location had changed on my system to a location that no longer existed, i updated this in the registry and the issue is gone.
for those who want to repeat the issue, here is what i changed:

In the Registry Editor go here:
Computer\HKEY_CURRENT_USER\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\User Shell Folders

Check the 'Cache' entry, does the path it has saved exist? or is it showing a weird path doesnt make sense?

change it instead to the following:
%USERPROFILE%\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Windows\INetCache

body: Visual Studio activation seems like an odd inclusion, as it isn't bundled with Windows by default; rather, it serves as external software utilized by programmers. If they introduce VS  activation as an add-on, it raises the question of potentially incorporating other arbitrary software activations as well. To maintain the tool's simplicity and consistency, it's best to limit its interaction solely to the pre-installed components that come with Windows.

> What product(s) are you trying to activate? If you have your own KMS server, why do you need the script? You should be able to configure your network so the KMS server is automatically found.

The system is windows 10 and windows 11, and the office series.
Our kms server is not official, it is set up using vlmcsd (https://github.com/Wind4/vlmcsd).
Because we have our own internal security software, we can't use methods like KMS38 or SppExtComobjHook.dll that require injecting files, so we can only use the online kms activation method.

author: WindowsAddict

body:
> It's already using WMI directly instead of slmgr.vbs tool for activation-related commands. In very few places, the script relies on slmgr.vbs, we can easily add alternative commands. Furthermore, I don't think they will make it unavailable by default, they will most likely treat it as they did with wmic.exe, available by default but can be removed.

The bigger idea is to move towards deprecating all VBS related functions and calls as there are PS equivalents of  both slmgr.vbs and ospp.vbs capabilities that can be used instead without regressions or loss of function. I am glad to hear these scripts will be replaced. 

Someone made a "drop in" replacement to the usual scripts that made use of slmgr.vbs functions. The major command doing the heavy lifting is the new CIM, Get-CimInstance. Found here: https://gist.github.com/zbalkan/4ba92656a3a8387e6b220bcf8fcd5fc6

Only difference between WMI and CIM is on how it communicates with remote systems. WMI is allegedly deprecated because it is windows only, while CIM can be multiplatform.

OSPP is similar but it is specific to office. The Get-CimInstance command can still work for this purpose. website with decent documented Powershell equivalents https://woshub.com/checking-office-2016-365-activation-status/

author: CYAsistemas

body:
There is no validation during the installation of RDS licenses.
- Open "Remote Desktop Licensing Manager"
- Right click the server and click "Activate Server"
- Select the connection method of choice and click next (default is fine)
- Fill the first company information with garbage and click next
- Leave the next page blank and click next
- The wizard should complete and say "The license server has been successfully activated"
- Keep "Start Install Licenses Wizard" checked and click next until it asks you to select a "License program"
- Change the License Program to "Service Provider License Agreement" in the dropdown and click next
- Set the agreement number to 1234567 and click next
- Fill out the next page with your chosen values and click next
- The license wizard should report "The requested licenses have been successfully installed"

Feel free to hop in the discord server if you have any issues.

author: Obegg

body:
Hi @Obegg 
We recommend not using debloating scripts and modded ISO and Windows butchering tools available on the Internet, instead, we prefer to deal with it in official ways. 

If you want to block telemetry then use the Enterprise edition of Windows and it will let you [block the telemetry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/privacy/manage-connections-from-windows-operating-system-components-to-microsoft-services#1816-feedback--diagnostics) with the security option in group policy.

If you don't want start menu ads then use Professional workstation or Enterprise editions. 

If you don't want preinstalled UWP apps then use Enterprise LTSC editions.
author: WindowsAddict

body:
I understand that Enterprise is great, but even that is not enough, windows still collects information even using Enterprise.
One example (out of many) is:
If you search for any settings in Windows 10 Enterprise it will send a DNS query to bing.com,
while it doesn't affect performance, it is a privacy/security issue for some.
Even the security option in group policy doesn't change that.
Windows 10 Enterprise is not perfect out of the box, some changes must be done.
author: Obegg

body:
> I understand that Enterprise is great, but even that is not enough, windows still collects information even using Enterprise. One example (out of many) is: If you search for any settings in Windows 10 Enterprise it will send a DNS query to bing.com, while it doesn't affect performance, it is a privacy/security issue for some. Even the security option in group policy doesn't change that. Windows 10 Enterprise is not perfect out of the box, some changes must be done.

That's not related to so called "debloating", or as I taken to calling it "mindlessly removing things and checking if it still boots". Group Policy is enough to make Windows silent and there is an [official documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/privacy/manage-connections-from-windows-operating-system-components-to-microsoft-services) for this.

It is an issue, simply because there's nothing that should ping bing, I even removed the edge browser, disabled news feeds, disabled everything I can, removed everything I can, there's nothing on the PC - still pings bing.

Probably encrypted and I wouldn't know.

> That's not related to so called "debloating", or as I taken to calling it "mindlessly removing things and checking if it still boots". Point is - windows is never silent, prove me wrong (hint: I use Pi-Hole so I know)
author: Obegg

body:
> It is an issue, simply because there's nothing that should ping bing, I even removed the edge browser, disabled news feeds, disabled everything I can, removed everything I can, there's nothing on the PC - still pings bing.

Bing is Microsoft owned and settings contains some dynamic data, it could be pulling that from bing. You can MITM the https using fiddler to check what is sent. Just because a DNS query exists does not make it an issue.

@Dusk-Golem 
I guess you want to use ohook 0.4 because it sets Resiliency on every run and with current ohook script, you need to run it again on new user accounts. 
I've made some changes in script and now it will ensure that Resiliency key is auto set on new user accounts creation and it will be created on current all user accounts as well. Check here [Ohook_Activation_AIO.zip](https://github.com/massgravel/Microsoft-Activation-Scripts/files/13793788/Ohook_Activation_AIO.zip)

I don't think you should need to use ohook 0.4 now. Let me know why would you still want to run 0.4 version.

---

Regarding the questions you have raised, you can find how those .dll files are compiled here https://massgrave.dev/ohook.html#Custom_sppcdll_Info 
Along with changing the base64 string, you will also need to change offset value here https://github.com/massgravel/Microsoft-Activation-Scripts/blob/master/MAS/Separate-Files-Version/Activators/Ohook_Activation_AIO.cmd#L871-#L872 which I'll need to check.

author: nguyenbaodanh

body:
@fenhaizi 
It looks like they have removed gatherosstate.exe in the latest Windows insider ISO's, which means upon upgrades, normal KMS (180 days) and KMS38 both activation's grave period won't be carried over to the new build, and users will have to run the activation again.
author: WindowsAddict

body:
> @fenhaizi It looks like they have removed gatherosstate.exe in the latest Windows insider ISO's, which means upon upgrades, normal KMS (180 days) and KMS38 both activation's grave period won't be carried over to the new build, and users will have to run the activation again.

1. It seems that only Server Insiders would lose KMS/KMS38 activation after upgrading, desktop insider versions have no problems.
2. For server insiders, after KMS/KMS38 activation, the system is in VOLUME_KMS channel, but after an upgrade, it becomes RETAIL channel.
3. Maybe you have already known that starting from a day in 2024, Windows server insiders can also install upgrades via Windows Update, just as desktops, no longer needed to download a new ISO and install it manually. I don't know whether it would bring the gatherosstate.exe back.
author: fenhaizi

body: gatherosstate is only removed from windows insider builds, it is still there on the older windows server releases

Open powershell as admin and enter

`Test-Path -Path "$env:windir\WinSxS\pending.xml"`
`Get-ChildItem "HKLM:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Component Based Servicing\RebootPending"`

author: YawanYadav

body:
`C:\Windows\WinSxS\pending.xml`

Your system has this file, and its causing the error. System automatically removes this file after system restart. 
Try to run windows update and install all the updates and restart system a couple of times, it should fix it. 
If not, please share that file here.

body:
@ladymacbethh98 
Changing license to volume or retail or OEM only requires entering the corresponding channel key in the windows activation page in settings. 

To view the OEM license key for windows that came with your purchased laptop, open command prompt as admin and enter

`wmic path softwarelicensingservice get oa3xoriginalproductkey`

It will show you a key, enter that key in the windows activation page in settings, and voila, the information there will change.
author: WindowsAddict

Thank you from your answer. 

I bought a laptop with - as I assumed - genuine Windows 11 from a genuine shop and installed Office 16 using this tool when I first opened it. Last day I noticed that my Windows license is Volume Key, not OEM. I was told by some professionals that sometimes the OEM version can be converted to Volume Key by some damaged update or by some file. And as far as I remember your script was the only "not genuine" file I've used. So my question is, could this activator trigger this conversion? I really appreciate your work but I need this information because the laptop will be repaired under warranty. 

I checked BIOS and there's a different key than in PowerShell so it's a crazy situation.
author: ladymacbethh98

body:
@ladymacbethh98 MAS will use the KMS (Volume) key for Windows if you mistakenly used the KMS38 option and still proceeded to activate even when it said Windows is already activated and activation isn't required.

If you didn't do that and only used ohook option for office, then no. MAS won't change the Windows key then. 

----

Btw, those professionals are wrong about that key can be changed to volume by update or by some file, it changes when some Volume key in inserted by someone/tool.

body:
@ckw-123 
I tested it in my system, and after KMS38 activation, the sppsvc service automatically closes itself after a few minutes.
Try restart and wait for some minutes, to see if there is any difference. 

If issue is still not resolved, open command prompt as admin and enter

`reg query "HKEY_USERS\S-1-5-20\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\SoftwareProtectionPlatform\PersistedSystemState"`

`reg query "HKEY_USERS\S-1-5-20\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\SoftwareProtectionPlatform"`

share screenshot. 

and after that, 

`reg delete "HKEY_USERS\S-1-5-20\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\SoftwareProtectionPlatform"`

and restart system and see if there is any change.

@ckw-123 Things are wrong in your registry

body:
in my case it wasn't dns, win 11 24h2 with latest patches only uses tls 1.3 by default and the domain in question doesn't support it:
- `[Net.ServicePointManager]::SecurityProtocol` only returns Tls13, and not Tls12 anymore
- ssllabs confirms tls 1.3 is not enabled: <https://ssllabs.com/ssltest/analyze.html?d=get.activated.win>

i had to do set tls1.2 additionally to make it work:
```pwsh
$spm = [Net.ServicePointManager]
$spt = [Net.SecurityProtocolType]
$spm::SecurityProtocol = $spm::SecurityProtocol -bor $spt::Tls12 -bor $spt::Tls13

irm https://get.activated.win | iex
```

you might want to enable tls 1.3 on this domain (it's standard by now and not new anymore) or update the readme

You need to explain exactly how MAS could be causing issues, or at least clear instructions so people can reproduce the issue in a VM. So far you've just given vague statements about slowness and output from a script so broken that  it literally showed an empty file as malware. We don't have access to your PC so we have no idea what you could be doing that caused the issues you are reporting.

author: WindowsAddict

body:
> - You can activate Windows with HWID and Office with ohook option. Office activation with any method in MAS doesn't invalidate HWID for Windows.
> - HWID license is not possible for Office, it doesn't exist. 

So in other words, HWID license cannot be applied to Office because Office activation status is gained from server side and an Microsoft account already needs to have linked license/product key that will be downloaded by Office programs and if valid to flag Office apps as activated.


So, everything is server-side while Windows 10/11 provides ability to trick activation status, sent it to servers and servers will automatically in server-side generate an digital license for specific hardware and link it to Microsoft account so that everytime if the hardware is the same can be automatically re-activated with digital license (simply via connecting to the internet and linking Microsoft account with PC)

author: pasha-zzz

body:
It was not done because,
- Relying on one server alone is not a enough reliable method to ensure lifetime activation
- Script is using KMS IP instead of KMS host name because MS has detected 2 KMS server names, https://massgrave.dev/office-license-is-not-genuine#reasons 
- Using IP instead of domain further more lowers the probability of lifetime activation because a server may change the IP

Based on these points, the renewal task was added to the script.

author: ghost

body:
@Waingro24 

You mentioned that you used Ventoy, it's possible that it's somehow causing that issue where that image is not appearing as per your screenshot, otherwise Home license in the motherboard won't cause the setup to throw that error.

---

You can use this universal ei,cfg format, which will work on any Windows version and edition (except the new installer in Windows 11 24H2, which is not accepting ei.cfg at all due to some bug.)

```
[Channel]
NoKeyChannel
```

PID format
```
[PID]
Value=XXXXX-XXXXX-XXXXX-XXXXX-XXXXX
```
Where XXXXX-XXXXX-XXXXX-XXXXX-XXXXX is the product key.

You can pick ei.efg or PID or both.
ei.cfg will allow you to see the edition list while installing Windows, and PID.txt will directly install that edition which key you used.

---

5, 6 
You can install key when Windows is installed, or you can install key at setupcomplete.cmd stage.
PID.txt won't work because Iot edition is not included in the ISO index, it will be available when Windows is installed.

7, avoid language packs if you can, use ISO in that language if possible.

8, shouldn't matter.

9, I think you can use both, not fully sure though.

10, use this tool https://massgrave.dev/oem-folder#edit-iso-file

author: ghost

body:
@Waingro24 
As mentioned, you can't use the Iot Enterprise LTSC key in PID.txt in non-English LTSC ISO because the IoT edition doesn't exist in the index. 

In this case, if you want to automate the process of installing IoT edition with non-English ISO, the easiest way would be to use setupcomple.cmd file.

`\sources\$OEM$\$$\Setup\Scripts\SetupComplete.cmd"`

Create these folders and that file in ISO and add the below command in that file.

`powershell "(([WMISEARCHER]'SELECT Version FROM SoftwareLicensingService').Get()).InstallProductKey('QPM6N-7J2WJ-P88HH-P3YRH-YY74H')"`

Now when Windows installation is finished, you will see it's installed as IoT enterprise edition.

---

If you aren't looking for such automation, you can just manually install the IoT edition key when install is finished.

body:
@moriel5 
Support can be added but it would require many additional steps. Besides, Microsoft doesn't want people to use it, and its built-in Office updater will switch to the C2R version by removing the UWP version and installing C2R instead. That is why I didn't bother supporting it.
author: WindowsAddict

body:
Thanks for the quick answer.
That is certainly surprising, since aside from a few (very few) hiccups, I have not seen any issues over the past 6+ years when activating via OfficeRTool (and I have installed and activated this specific Office suite many times over that timeperiod, for pretty much anyone who I set up a Windows PC (desktop, laptop, tablet) for, which is a few tens of people, at a ratio of around 2.5 devices per person (some more), including clean reinstallations with newer Windows 10 versions.
author: moriel5

body: @moriel5 how are you installing UWP Office?
author: WindowsAddict

body:
Manually.
I download the packages via the ADGuard website interface to the store (the CategoryID is required), and install them via the graphical Package Installer (I still need to find time to figure out how to conduct a system-wide installation), as the individual app packages have to be installed simultaneously, and right after the base package.

To activate them, I convert the license model from C2R to VL, and activate via KMS (as ohook wasn't present in the OfficeRTool version I was using last time I needed to do so, or I did not notice the addition).

And aside from extremely rare situations (perhaps 5 in total over the past 6+ years, on two occasions), updates have never broken the activation status.
author: moriel5

body:
About that auto-uninstall thing, that seems to be happening when C2R Office is installed side by side.
Btw, any specific reason you use this over C2R Office?
To me, it's just an inferior version of Office, and I don't see why anyone needs to use it.
author: WindowsAddict

body:
Again, I am surprised, as I see no difference between the two, except that this version is much more easier to manage, as a technician, and draws less system resources (marginally, there are less background services, as all updates are managed via the store, which do work automatically as expected), and leaves next to no residue, even without scrubbing (so I can easily uninstall and reinstall without any worries in regards to what was there first).

Also, whether activating unofficially or officially (whether by standard license or by account, either of which determines the version and edition), there is no functional difference that I have perceived (yes, I have done both, depending upon whether it was a customer or someone I personally knew who was alright with it).
author: moriel5

body: @moriel5 I'll add support for it in the KMS script, but it's not possible in Ohook because Office updates are removing all files and creating a new folder.

Logout from Office apps
Remove every account from the credential manager

```
rd /s /q "%localappdata%\Microsoft\OneAuth"
rd /s /q "%localappdata%\Microsoft\IdentityCache"
rd /s /q "%localappdata%\Microsoft\Office"
reg delete HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Office /f
```
and then log in and check
Share results.

body:
This is a normal behavior with KMS activation. 
The system attempts to renew the activation at certain intervals, it tries to renew activation when you connect to the internet or when you log in to the system. 
You can ignore this, this is a cosmetic error and happens even in a genuine KMS environment. 

It didn't turn off your Internet.

body:
The script is open-source. Random sandbox website runs are therefore not proof that the script is malware.

The reasons for why any.run is flagging MAS as malware is preposterous.

Without even reading the code, you can figure out why the first point happens. It's because MAS will almost 99% of the time need to relaunch itself after being initially launched to set things like QuickEdit settings or to run itself as administrator. You can see this by running the script yourself in a VM.

For the second point, MAS uses `net` commands to check whether Windows services are running correctly or not, or to start them. That's basically all it does. You can CTRL + F `net` in the code and check for yourself.

If you look closely, any.run is also flagging Google Chrome as having malicious behavior because you opened the script through Chrome and tripped the "opened cmd.exe" check. Do you understand why these detections mean nothing?

I will reiterate again. **The script is open-source.** The code for the script is right there. You can literally CTRL + F `sc` for example and see what the script is using it for

body:
> Also why did you stop attaching source code to the commits since 2.5?

He did not. The source code on GitHub will always need to be updated as the bootstrapper script for launching MAS (as seen in https://get.activated.win) uses GitHub as one of the sources for downloading the script.
author: thecatontheceiling

body:
> Also why did you stop attaching source code to the commits since 2.5?

The source code is the final script. It was removed in release section to avoid antivirus detections on the same hash file. When you download it from main, you get the latest file, and the latest files don't have antivirus detection that often, detection happens when the file gets old and AVs learn that this specific file is an activator and they then start flagging it.

body:
It's not possible to fully reverse the Confirmation ID algorithm for modern Windows because it requires having the private keys Microsoft uses to generate these CID's

The above is also true for Windows XP, but the private keys used in XP were much shorter and much more feasible to bruteforce compared to the key in use for modern Windows, you'd need to use some kind of supercomputer
author: thecatontheceiling

body:
Have you tested if Office works before you do anything Ohook related?

Either way I personally don't think sandboxing Office is a reasonable thing to do. Office 2019 still has support, just keep it up to date.

The CVE you linked to was fixed _2 years ago_, and was privately disclosed to Microsoft and fixed before the existence of it became public. These high severity vulnerabilities for Office come around very rarely and are fixed extremely quickly by Microsoft
author: thecatontheceiling

body:
If you insist, we still cannot help you with this, Ohook (and by extension Office) requires access to Windows licensing components, even before the Office installer is finished. If sandboxing doesn't allow for this kind of access to these components, we cannot help you.

I'd suggest just installing Office outside of a sandbox instead.

> if you're really desperate although I'd suggest just installing Office outside of a sandbox instead.

Nope, that will leave C2R outside the sandbox and is very dangerous, basically opening holes in the sanboxie by allowing a bunch of IPC of C2R.
author: giiutfff

body:
> Office 2019 has support left until October 2025, Office 2021 has support until October 2026

That's less than a year, and Office 2021 does not even install in sandbox.

BTW, You guys reply **_fast_** 🤣
author: giiutfff

body:
No, you can't just "remove" dependencies like that without resorting to extremely hacky things and without facing issues. Unfortunately Microsoft Office wasn't designed with that in mind.

If you're really this paranoid, I'm pretty sure there's a free online version of Office available by Microsoft somewhere, I used to use it for some time before Ohook was a thing. Again I'll just say, in my opinion, there's very little reason to be this paranoid.

You can also look into Microsoft Office alternatives that would likely work under a sandbox (OnlyOffice, LibreOffice, etc.).

body:
> No, you can't just "remove" dependencies like that without resorting to extremely hacky things and without facing issues. Unfortunately Microsoft Office wasn't designed with that in mind.

I thought Ohook is already extremely hacky, because it does not need a KMS or anything like that.

> If you're really this paranoid, I'm pretty sure there's a free online version of Office available by Microsoft somewhere, I used to use it for some time before Ohook was a thing. Again I'll just say, in my opinion, there's very little reason to be this paranoid.
>

As I said some files are vba enabled, and non of these apps support it.

> Just virtualize office instead of relying on some shitty sandboxing software which exposes a huge attack surface

That is actually doable, but with a huge overhead. And moving files in and out of the VM is also a headache. That is why I am trying to use sandbox. And sandbox is NOT shitty, provided you configure it **_correctly_**.
author: giiutfff

body:
VMWare is pretty fast for all of my tests (even when I allocate it a whopping two cores). I'm certain Office would work fine lol. It's definitely better than trusting your sandbox to keep you safe. It's inherently a lot more insecure than virtualization and would not be a bad alternative considering you're very paranoid about the files you're opening 

Sandboxes are "shitty" because it's not possible to completely isolate anything on Windows without involving some kind of virtualization.

> It's definitely better than trusting your sandbox to keep you safe. It's inherently a lot more insecure than virtualization and would not be a bad alternative considering you're very paranoid about the files you're opening

That is why I do not want to install Office out side of sandbox and run it in side (BTW it work absolutely fine this way). To prevent IPC holes 🤔, I need to install everything inside the sandbox.

I do not think there is any virtualization. It just replaces some system service and blocks the rest. And yes, things like screen capture is not blocked, but I just firewall it, so that is not a privacy issue.

body:
Start command prompt as administrator, copy / paste and run the following:
```
reg delete HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Office\16.0 /f
reg delete HKCU\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Office\16.0 /f
reg delete HKCU\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Cloud\Office\16.0 /f
reg delete HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Office\16.0 /f
reg delete HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Cloud\Office\16.0 /f
reg delete HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Office\16.0 /f /reg:32
reg delete HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Cloud\Office\16.0 /f /reg:32

```

body: The master branch download is hosted by GitHub and directly downloads the source code hosted here, so it would be impossible for it to include any malware unless that malware was also present in the source code hosted here. Anyone can simply read through the scripts here to ensure there is nothing malicious, and many people have, so any malware issues did not originate from this repo. If you have a malware infection, follow our [malware removal guide](https://gravesoft.dev/remove_malware) and reach out on the discord if you need any more help. If you have any security concerns with the script itself or anything MASSGRAVE, please ask us privately about it first so we can clear up any misunderstandings.

body:
> Windows XP SP2 Pro ENG won't accept a valid Windows XP SP2 Pro German key

Incorrect.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/97f46737-a27c-4d79-bddd-26c924426b61)

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/37839b68-a01a-4a71-b506-b6923bc36e71)

The only locale-dependent keys are for K/KN editions. 

OEM keys are also incompatible with Retail ISOs and vice versa, though any OEM iso can be converted to Retail by editing `I386\SETUPP.INI` and replacing `OEM` with `000`.

> there is NO working Windows XP retail/oem keygen for that matter

https://mskt.surge.sh

key generation parameters can be found at https://umskt.github.io/PIDDatabase-viewer/

body:
**`KB5058499`** (Cumulative Update Preview for Windows 11 Version 24H2 LTSC) has modified the licensing system (SPPSVC- Software Protection Platform Service) effecting MAK for the TSforge offline method of permanent activation.

Given the MAK key is deleted after activation, Microsoft assumes customers or end users won't need their full MAK key AFTER activation, but in reality, issues like hardware failures, reinstalls and unexpected update conflicts (KB5058499) can result in activation errors.

-------------

Has anyone tried backing up the licensing records before applying KB5058499?

1) Copy the entire spp\ **store** (folder).
`C:\Windows\System32\spp\store`

2) Export → Save the file as a .reg
`HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\SoftwareProtectionPlatform`

After installing **KB5058499**, if activation fails:

1) Go to `C:\Windows\System32\spp\store` and replace the updated files with your backup.

2) Then **MERGE** the .reg file to restore the previous registry values when activation was working properly.

3) Command Prompt (Admin): **`slmgr /rilc`**

This reinstates the previous activation status by reloading the previous activation information as I understand.

4) **Verify Activation** - This will confirm whether Windows is still activated afterwards: **`slmgr /xpr`**


> Note: This assumes Microsoft hasn't fundamentally changed how activation works in KB5058499, (e.g., enforcing _revalidation_). 
> 
> Note: If reactivation is still required after restoring files, you may need a new MAK key.

-------------

Unfortunately, to obtain your MAK Product Key running in command prompt:

**`wmic path softwarelicensingservice get OA3xOriginalProductKey`**

> Isn't supported in Windows 11 build 24H2. 

The Microsoft Store app "**`ShowKeyPlus`**" doesn't function for MAK keys (because they are deleted after activation. 

However, in Powershell: 

**`(Get-WmiObject -query "select * from SoftwareLicensingService").OA3xOriginalProductKey`**

-or-

**`(Get-CimInstance -query "select * from SoftwareLicensingService").OA3xOriginalProductKey`**

> This returns only the original product key, but not the deleted active MAK key after activation. 

**`ProduKey v1.97`** by www.nirsoft.net only displays the original product key, not the current active product key for MAK too. 

The free **`Hasleo version 5.2.2.3`** (www.hasleo.com) lets you backup the windows 11 24H2 operating system before applying KB5058499 by creating a full backup image that you can later restore back after applying KB5058499. 

> Of course, the Cumulative Update Preview for Windows 11 Version 24H2 can be uninstalled and then _activated just like before_ for anyone needing to know. After June 10, 2025 this preview becomes mandatory. 

-------------

Has anyone checked to see if Windows 11 24H2 is logging any relevant error messages about activation after KB5058499 (e.g. Error Code: `0xc004f031`)?

Error **`0xc004f031`** usually signifies that there is an activation problem where Windows cannot activate properly due to issues related to the product key or license validation.

> The Installation ID (IID) and the Confirmation ID (CID) do not match.

body:
StaticCID [already uses VAMT](https://github.com/dadorner-msft/activationws) to obtain the CID. If you want to type the CID manually, you can disconnect the internet, run `slui 4` in Command Prompt, and follow the onscreen instructions. There isn't much point in doing this though, you will just end up contacting Microsoft through a phone rather than over the internet.

The value `31337-42069-123-456789-04-1337-2600.0000-2542001` is a fake Extended PID. You can see a real one by running `slmgr.vbs /dlv`. The inclusion of values like `1337` and `123456789` is meant to show that this value is meaningless and serves no purpose, other than that the server expects some kind of EPID.
author: WitherOrNot

body:
In Windows 11 build 27802 and all versions after 26100.4188 (including current retail releases), Confirmation ID (CID) cache validation fails, forcing systems to revalidate the CID online every time license status is checked. This breaks all offline activation scenarios, which are critical for air-gapped or isolated systems.

Tools that depend on this cache no longer work, and activation for secure or disconnected clients becomes impossible.

This is affecting deployment and service workflows in enterprise environments, especially where compliance or operational constraints prohibit network connectivity.

We need CID cache validation to be restored, or a documented alternative to ensure reliable offline activation.

If others are facing the same issue, please upvote or comment to help raise visibility.

body:
Last time I tried it works but we heavily discourage people use custom OSs as they introduce heavy risk of instability. 

If you need a "lightweight" Windows version, you can get LTSC which is much more stable:
https://massgrave.dev/windows_ltsc_links

There's no guarantee that activation will continue to work as I've seen some custom OSs be able to activate successfully but then lose the activation after some time.
author: thecatontheceiling

body:
> they introduce heavy risk of instability.

can you explain this further?
author: throwaway242685

body:
Windows is not a lightweight operating system. There's nothing you can do to change that without breaking random things and causing instability. Windows does not work like Linux, where you can make stable "distros" with things removed.

Custom OSs like X-Lite mostly exist to farm donations from people who don't know that Windows is unsalvageable in this respect. We see reports of problems online caused by custom OSs all the time, and they're nigh impossible to troubleshoot due to the closed nature of these projects.

The best you can do is use some other operating system that will respect older hardware. If you insist on using Windows, LTSC is ideal. LTSC is made by Microsoft (so, in this case, people who actually know how Windows works), and you will most likely have a much better experience compared to using any custom OS or "normal" editions of Windows. You can get more info about it on the page I linked previously.

body:
Commercial ESU does not show anything in Settings or Windows Update

you just check licensing status in command prompt using: `slmgr /dli`
or the verbose status:
```
pushd %windir%\System32
cscript slmgr.vbs /dlv
```

body:
Similar question here.
I have an (official) OEM_DM Win10 Pro license and want to know if the VOLUME_MAK ESU licenses TSForge "creates" will work - Windows Update settings now say that I won't receive security updates anymore.

The only way I know to ckeck if those licenses are 'active' is entering "smlgr /dlv" into the command prompt, which lists all active licenses, but how do I know if windows actually recognizes them (i.e. continues getting security updates)
author: Gmochey

body:
If the `Client-ESU` licenses are activated, then Windows will get ESU updates. These licenses are all that is checked to determine if ESU is allowed to be installed.

You can check the status of these licenses with `slmgr /dlv` or `Check Activation Status` in MAS.
author: WitherOrNot

body:

body:
> sadly, if you use TSforge this message shows
> if you change it back to HWID the message vanishes and all works
> add back TSforge and it comes back
> 
> I would go as far as to say it's detecting the TSforge type key
> 

Updates are not disabled, Settings shows this because the Settings data server is not properly configured.

If you installed ESU via TSforge, you can check by running this command,

`reg query "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\SoftwareProtectionPlatform\ESU" /v Win10CommercialKeybasedESUEligible`

This is the value sent directly to Windows Update servers to fetch the ESU. As long as it says `0x1`, nothing else matters.
author: WitherOrNot

body:
https://massgrave.dev/faq#why-is-the-windows-update-page-in-windows-10-enterprise-ltsc-2021-showing-your-device-is-no-longer-receiving-security-updates

**Note:**  
Some so-called privacy tools may block connections to Microsoft services, which can prevent important features or updates from working properly. If you’ve used such tools, you may need to undo these changes.  

To do so, open Command Prompt as Administrator and enter the following commands:
```
reg delete "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\DataCollection" /v DisableOneSettingsDownloads /f

reg add "HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NlaSvc\Parameters\Internet" /v EnableActiveProbing /t REG_DWORD /d 1 /f
```
**Restart** the system, run “Check for updates” again. The end-of-support message should no longer appear.

body:
Have two questions around the Windows 10 ESU, if anyone can help:
___
**[1] For PCs that are already-activated, is it only required to run the TASforge option?**

- If a PC is already activated with its own (original) Digital Licence, then I assume you just run the TASforge option?
(Or is your specific .XML file required to be in the "GenuineTicket" folder for TASforge to work?)

- What about PCs that have been activated using an online MAK server?
Can you just run TASforge, or is it necessary for those to de-activate, then re-activate using the HWID method, first?
___
**[2] After TASforge has been ran, can you then update those PCs offline, or only via Windows Update?**

- So on PCs where TASforge has been successfully ran, can you manually install the .MSU updates from the [Microsoft Catalog](https://www.catalog.update.microsoft.com/Search.aspx?q=KB5068781+x64),
or for the extended-updates, is only online updating via Windows Update supported?

And for anyone who has tried installing an .MSU manually:

- Does double-clicking on them still work, or like in Windows 11, did you have to use:
DISM /Online /Add-Package /PackagePath:<path to .MSU file>
or
Add-WindowsPackage -Online -PackagePath "<path to .MSU file>"
?
___
Thanks

body:
@Alyaqdhans 
Your system is deactivating because you are using Windows to go; the hardware ID is going to change in that, and the system will deactivate because of that.

To fix it, use the TSforge option to activate it (when system is deactivated or run `slmgr /upk` command in cmd as admin before running TSforge). 

ZeroCID activation method used by TSforge on Windows 10 can stay even after hardware change.

---

About the issue of the PowerShell command not working, use method 2 listed in the README to use MAS.

Follow these steps, and if the issue persists,
- Open the credential manager and ensure that your Microsoft account is removed from there.
- Check if there is any work account attached to your Windows account; if yes, go to settings and see if it's properly logged in.

If the issue persists,
- Run Ohook and share screenshot of the output
- Share screenshot of where Office is asking for login, and go to account page in Office apps and share screenshot of that.

body:
@VorgonBattlecruiser 

```
sc.exe qc NlaSvc
sc.exe qc netprofm
sc.exe query NlaSvc
sc.exe query netprofm
Restart-Service NlaSvc -Force
Restart-Service netprofm -Force
```

Enter this in powershell as admin, share screenshot.
After that restart system and see if it fixes the issue.
author: WindowsAddict

body:
I had no idea any software would ever actually require the network status indicator.
i thought just working, unblocked internet access would be enough.
Is it really necessary to check the network status indicator when you COULD just simply access the internet?
Your scripts even recognized i had internet... and then just did not feel like actually doing it.

for anyone googling this:
i used the privacy.sexy scripts to block the status indicator months ago on windows 10, it didnt do anything, forgot about it.
remembered it today after it ended up working great on windows 11 and the upgrade from 10 to 11 didnt fix it... it activated it.
there is a function to revert it in privacy.sexy, which didnt work.
i copied the entire script of changes privacy.sexy made (right side of the window next to the options to select your chosen changes) into ChatGPT, it recognized it and walked me through undoing it all.
author: VorgonBattlecruiser

body: Script only asks the system to perform the activation; actual activation is done by the system. Windows doesn't do many Internet-related things if it can't find the Internet connection.

body: i will! but be prepared, this was not an easy or quick process, ill only share what i think finally fixed it and give the relevant chatgpt log at the end. TLDR first: Youve seen what my registry under HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NlaSvc\Parameters\Internet looked like, now its this: there were also entries in the hosts-file i cleaned, folders deleted where NCSI stored data etc. it wasnt pretty. The entire Log: Skip to content Chat history You said: my windows 11 network status indicator doesnt work. All values in regedit under "Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NlaSvc\Parameters\Internet" are as expected. "www.msftconnecttest.com" isnt blocked in my hosts-file or my router. i tethered my phone and skipped my router entirely. ChatGPT said: Got it! Since you’ve already checked the registry keys under NlaSvc and the connectivity test domain isn’t blocked, and you’ve even bypassed your router, let’s carefully go through the likely causes and troubleshooting steps for why Windows 11’s network status indicator might not work: 1. Check NLA Service The Network Location Awareness (NLA) service drives the status indicator. Make sure it’s running and set to automatic: Press Win + R, type services.msc, and press Enter. Find Network Location Awareness. Make sure: Status: Running Startup type: Automatic If not running, right-click → Start. Also check Network List Service, which depends on NLA. 2. Check Connectivity Status Even if msftconnecttest.com is reachable, Windows may be failing the “active probe” for some reason. Let’s test it: Open Command Prompt and run You said: i ran all these commands as well and nothing fixed it: reg add "HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Internet Settings" /v ProxyEnable /t REG_DWORD /d 0 /f reg add "HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Internet Settings" /v ProxyServer /t REG_SZ /d "" /f reg add "HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NlaSvc\Parameters\Internet" /v EnableActiveProbing /t REG_DWORD /d 1 /f $filePath = "$env:SystemRoot\System32\drivers\etc\hosts" Set-Content -Path $filePath -Value (Get-Content $filePath | ForEach-Object { $_ -replace '.*licensing.mp.microsoft.com.*', ''}) -force Set-Content -Path $filePath -Value (Get-Content $filePath | ForEach-Object { $_ -replace '.*purchase.mp.microsoft.com.*', ''}) -force Set-Content -Path $filePath -Value (Get-Content $filePath | ForEach-Object { $_ -replace '.*login.live.com.*', ''}) -force Set-Content -Path $filePath -Value (Get-Content $filePath | ForEach-Object { $_ -replace '.*msftconnecttest.*', ''}) -force Set-Content -Path $filePath -Value (Get-Content $filePath | ForEach-Object { $_ -replace '.*msftncsi.*', ''}) -force netsh int ip reset netsh winsock reset netsh advfirewall reset netsh winhttp reset proxy bitsadmin /util /setieproxy localsystem NO_PROXY RESET ipconfig /flushdns ChatGPT said: Thanks, that helps clarify the situation. You’ve basically done all the usual registry, hosts, proxy, and network stack resets—so we’re now in a rarer category: Windows thinks it has no Internet even when it does, which usually comes down to NLA, DNS, or some service-level corruption. Let’s narrow it down step by step. 1. Test NCSI manually Windows uses NCSI (Network Connectivity Status Indicator) to decide if the network is “connected.” Let’s see if the probe itself works: Open a browser and go to: http://www.msftconnecttest.com/connecttest.txt You should see plain text: Microsoft Connect Test Open PowerShell and run: Invoke-WebRequest http://www.msftconnecttest.com/connecttest.txt If it fails, Windows cannot reach the test URL—even if the browser works. Sometimes firewalls or VPNs block the NLA probe even on a working connection. 2. Check DNS resolution Sometimes the NCSI probe fails because of DNS weirdness, even on tethered connections: nslookup www.msftconnecttest.com ping www.msftconnecttest.com If nslookup works but ping fails, it might be a firewall blocking ICMP, which is normal. But if DNS fails, NLA can’t detect connectivity. 3. Check Event Viewer for NLA errors Press Win + X → Event Viewer → Applications and Services Logs → Microsoft → Windows → NlaSvc → Operational Look for errors like “NCSI failed to probe Internet connectivity”. This often tells you exactly what Windows is failing on. 4. Try a direct NLA reset Sometimes the NLA profile itself is corrupted: # Stop services Stop-Service NlaSvc -Force Stop-Service nlasvc /Force # Delete NLA cache Remove-Item "C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Network\Nla\Cache" -Recurse -Force # Restart services Start-Service NlaSvc Then reboot and see if the status icon updates. 5. Check for Group Policy or System-wide blocking Even on a clean tether: Run gpedit.msc → Computer Configuration → Administrative Templates → Network → Network Connectivity Status Indicator Ensure “Turn off Windows Network Connectivity Status Indicator active tests” is Not Configured. At this stage, if none of these work, the issue is usually a corruption in the NLA service or the system network stack itself, which can sometimes only be fixed by a repair install (Settings → Recovery → Reset this PC → Keep my files)—but we can try a few last tricks first. If you want, I can give a targeted hack that forces Windows to trust Internet connectivity, bypassing NCSI entirely, which often fixes the indicator without touching the rest of the network. Do you want me to do that next? You said: http://www.msftconnecttest.com/connecttest.txt shows "Microsoft Connect Test". please only one step at a time ChatGPT said: Perfect—that confirms that NCSI can reach its test URL, so the issue isn’t DNS or external blocking. Next step: let’s check if the NLA service is actually reporting connectivity correctly. Open PowerShell as Administrator and run: Get-NetConnectionProfile Look at the output for your active network. Focus on: NetworkCategory (Public / Private / Domain) IPv4Connectivity and IPv6Connectivity Can you tell me what values it shows? You said: PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> Get-NetConnectionProfile >> Name : Network InterfaceAlias : Ethernet InterfaceIndex : 15 NetworkCategory : Private DomainAuthenticationKind : None IPv4Connectivity : NoTraffic IPv6Connectivity : NoTraffic ChatGPT said: Ah—that’s very telling. Even though your Internet works and NCSI can reach its URL, Windows thinks there’s “NoTraffic” on both IPv4 and IPv6. That’s why the network status icon stays “disconnected” or “limited.” This usually points to NLA being unable to correctly detect traffic on the interface. Since you’re tethering your phone, some peculiarities of the tethered adapter can trigger this. Let’s try the simplest forced refresh of NLA’s network profile first: Open PowerShell as Admin. Run this command to reset the network category and trigger a re-evaluation: Set-NetConnectionProfile -InterfaceAlias "Ethernet" -NetworkCategory Private After running it, run Get-NetConnectionProfile again and see if IPv4Connectivity or IPv6Connectivity changes from NoTraffic to Internet. Do this and tell me what it shows. You said: Windows PowerShell Copyright (C) Microsoft Corporation. All rights reserved. Install the latest PowerShell for new features and improvements! https://aka.ms/PSWindows PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> Get-NetConnectionProfile >> Name : Network InterfaceAlias : Ethernet InterfaceIndex : 15 NetworkCategory : Private DomainAuthenticationKind : None IPv4Connectivity : NoTraffic IPv6Connectivity : NoTraffic PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> Set-NetConnectionProfile -InterfaceAlias "Ethernet" -NetworkCategory Private >> PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> Get-NetConnectionProfile >> Name : Network InterfaceAlias : Ethernet InterfaceIndex : 15 NetworkCategory : Private DomainAuthenticationKind : None IPv4Connectivity : NoTraffic IPv6Connectivity : NoTraffic PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> ChatGPT said: Okay—that confirms Windows isn’t updating the network profile at all. Set-NetConnectionProfile didn’t trigger any change in IPv4Connectivity or IPv6Connectivity. Next step: force NLA to re-probe the network by restarting its service and clearing its cache. This is usually the step that fixes “NoTraffic” on tethered or unusual adapters. Run these commands in PowerShell as Administrator: # Stop NLA service Stop-Service NlaSvc -Force # Remove cached profiles Remove-Item "C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Network\Nla\Cache" -Recurse -Force # Start NLA service Start-Service NlaSvc After doing this, wait 10–15 seconds and then run: Get-NetConnectionProfile to see if IPv4Connectivity or IPv6Connectivity has changed. Do this and report the output. You said: Windows PowerShell Copyright (C) Microsoft Corporation. All rights reserved. Install the latest PowerShell for new features and improvements! https://aka.ms/PSWindows PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> Get-NetConnectionProfile >> Name : Network InterfaceAlias : Ethernet InterfaceIndex : 15 NetworkCategory : Private DomainAuthenticationKind : None IPv4Connectivity : NoTraffic IPv6Connectivity : NoTraffic PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> Set-NetConnectionProfile -InterfaceAlias "Ethernet" -NetworkCategory Private >> PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> Get-NetConnectionProfile >> Name : Network InterfaceAlias : Ethernet InterfaceIndex : 15 NetworkCategory : Private DomainAuthenticationKind : None IPv4Connectivity : NoTraffic IPv6Connectivity : NoTraffic PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> Stop-Service NlaSvc -Force PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> Remove-Item "C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Network\Nla\Cache" -Recurse -Force Remove-Item : Cannot find path 'C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Network\Nla\Cache' because it does not exist. At line:1 char:1 + Remove-Item "C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Network\Nla\Cache" -Recurse -Fo ... + ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ + CategoryInfo : ObjectNotFound: (C:\ProgramData\...twork\Nla\Cache:String) [Remove-Item], ItemNotFoundEx ception + FullyQualifiedErrorId : PathNotFound,Microsoft.PowerShell.Commands.RemoveItemCommand PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> ChatGPT said: Got it—the fact that C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Network\Nla\Cache doesn’t exist is unusual. That folder normally stores NLA’s cached network profiles. Its absence may be why NLA isn’t detecting traffic properly. Next step: recreate the NLA cache folder and restart the service to force Windows to regenerate it. Run these commands in PowerShell as Administrator: # Recreate NLA cache folder New-Item -Path "C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Network\Nla\Cache" -ItemType Directory -Force # Set proper permissions for NLA service $acl = Get-Acl "C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Network\Nla\Cache" $rule = New-Object System.Security.AccessControl.FileSystemAccessRule("NETWORK SERVICE","FullControl","ContainerInherit,ObjectInherit","None","Allow") $acl.SetAccessRule($rule) Set-Acl "C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Network\Nla\Cache" $acl # Restart NLA service Restart-Service NlaSvc -Force After that, wait ~10–15 seconds and then run: Get-NetConnectionProfile Check if IPv4Connectivity now shows Internet instead of NoTraffic. This step often fixes tethered connections that Windows refuses to recognize. You said: PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> $acl = Get-Acl "C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Network\Nla\Cache" PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> $rule = New-Object System.Security.AccessControl.FileSystemAccessRule("NETWORK SERVICE","FullControl","ContainerInherit,ObjectInherit","None","Allow") PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> $acl.SetAccessRule($rule) PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> Set-Acl "C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Network\Nla\Cache" $acl PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> Restart-Service NlaSvc -Force PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> Get-NetConnectionProfile >> Name : Network InterfaceAlias : Ethernet InterfaceIndex : 15 NetworkCategory : Private DomainAuthenticationKind : None IPv4Connectivity : NoTraffic IPv6Connectivity : NoTraffic PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> Get-NetConnectionProfile >> Name : Network InterfaceAlias : Ethernet InterfaceIndex : 15 NetworkCategory : Private DomainAuthenticationKind : None IPv4Connectivity : NoTraffic IPv6Connectivity : NoTraffic PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> ChatGPT said: Alright—this confirms it: NLA is completely failing to detect traffic on your tethered interface, even with a fresh cache folder and proper permissions. That’s why IPv4Connectivity and IPv6Connectivity remain NoTraffic. At this point, the problem is deep in how Windows is treating the Ethernet interface from your phone tether, not something that can be fixed by registry, cache, or service tweaks. The next step is to bypass NLA’s active probe entirely and force Windows to always consider this network “connected.” This is done by disabling the NCSI check in the registry. It won’t affect your actual Internet, but will fix the status indicator. Run this command in an elevated PowerShell: # Disable NCSI active probes New-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NlaSvc\Parameters\Internet" -Name "EnableActiveProbing" -PropertyType DWORD -Value 0 -Force Then restart your PC. After reboot, check: Get-NetConnectionProfile and see if IPv4Connectivity now shows Internet. Do you want me to walk through a slightly safer variant that keeps the probe but forces Windows to trust this network? It’s often better for tethered connections. You said: its treating ANY connection this way, not just tethered ChatGPT said: Ah—that’s a critical detail. If all networks are showing NoTraffic, not just your tethered phone, then this isn’t a tether-specific issue—it’s a system-wide NLA/NCSI detection failure. That explains why every network interface is stuck at NoTraffic. At this point, the problem is likely one of these system-level causes: Corrupted or missing NLA/NCSI components The NLA service itself, or the NCSI binaries/configuration, may be damaged. Windows relies on C:\Windows\System32\ncsi.dll and related system files. Firewall or security software blocking NLA probes Even Windows Firewall reset didn’t help, but some AV suites or endpoint security software can intercept NCSI probes system-wide. Corrupted network stack or system files Even after netsh int ip reset and winsock reset, deeper file-level corruption can cause this. Next step: Check system file integrity Run an elevated PowerShell / CMD and execute: sfc /scannow Wait until it finishes. If it finds errors it cannot fix, follow with: DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth These commands repair core Windows system files—including NLA/NCSI components. After that, reboot and check: Get-NetConnectionProfile If this doesn’t fix it, the next step would be a repair install of Windows 11 (keeping files) because the issue is at the OS level, not just a tweak. Do you want to proceed with sfc /scannow now? You said: no corruption found. i think this is the script i ran on windows 10 months ago before upgrading to windows 11 today to disable the network status indicator: @echo off :: https://privacy.sexy — v0.13.8 — Wed, 12 Nov 2025 16:30:12 GMT :: Ensure PowerShell is available where PowerShell >nul 2>&1 || ( echo PowerShell is not available. Please install or enable PowerShell. pause & exit 1 ) :: Ensure admin privileges fltmc >nul 2>&1 || ( echo Administrator privileges are required. PowerShell Start -Verb RunAs '%0' 2> nul || ( echo Right-click on the script and select "Run as administrator". pause & exit 1 ) exit 0 ) :: Initialize environment setlocal EnableExtensions DisableDelayedExpansion :: Disable active connectivity tests (breaks internet connection status, captive portals) echo --- Disable active connectivity tests (breaks internet connection status, captive portals) :: Set the registry value: "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\NetworkConnectivityStatusIndicator!NoActiveProbe" PowerShell -ExecutionPolicy Unrestricted -Command "$registryPath = 'HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\NetworkConnectivityStatusIndicator'; $data = '1'; reg add 'HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\NetworkConnectivityStatusIndicator' /v 'NoActiveProbe' /t 'REG_DWORD' /d "^""$data"^"" /f" :: Set the registry value: "HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NlaSvc\Parameters\Internet!EnableActiveProbing" PowerShell -ExecutionPolicy Unrestricted -Command "$registryPath = 'HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NlaSvc\Parameters\Internet'; $data = '0'; reg add 'HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NlaSvc\Parameters\Internet' /v 'EnableActiveProbing' /t 'REG_DWORD' /d "^""$data"^"" /f" :: Suggest restarting computer for changes to take effect PowerShell -ExecutionPolicy Unrestricted -Command "$message = 'For the changes to fully take effect, please restart your computer.'; $warn = $false; if ($warn) { Write-Warning "^""$message"^""; } else { Write-Host "^""Note: "^"" -ForegroundColor Blue -NoNewLine; Write-Output "^""$message"^""; }" :: ---------------------------------------------------------- :: Disable passive connectivity tests (breaks internet connection status) echo --- Disable passive connectivity tests (breaks internet connection status) :: Set the registry value: "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\NetworkConnectivityStatusIndicator!DisablePassivePolling" PowerShell -ExecutionPolicy Unrestricted -Command "$registryPath = 'HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\NetworkConnectivityStatusIndicator'; $data = '1'; reg add 'HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\NetworkConnectivityStatusIndicator' /v 'DisablePassivePolling' /t 'REG_DWORD' /d "^""$data"^"" /f" :: Set the registry value: "HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NlaSvc\Parameters\Internet!PassivePollPeriod" PowerShell -ExecutionPolicy Unrestricted -Command "$registryPath = 'HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NlaSvc\Parameters\Internet'; $data = '0'; reg add 'HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NlaSvc\Parameters\Internet' /v 'PassivePollPeriod' /t 'REG_DWORD' /d "^""$data"^"" /f" :: ---------------------------------------------------------- :: Remove "Network Connectivity Status Indicator (NCSI)" app (breaks internet connection status icon) echo --- Remove "Network Connectivity Status Indicator (NCSI)" app (breaks internet connection status icon) :: Enable removal of system app 'NcsiUwpApp' by marking it as "EndOfLife" :: Create "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Appx\AppxAllUserStore\EndOfLife\$CURRENT_USER_SID\NcsiUwpApp_8wekyb3d8bbwe" registry key PowerShell -ExecutionPolicy Unrestricted -Command "$keyPath='HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Appx\AppxAllUserStore\EndOfLife\$CURRENT_USER_SID\NcsiUwpApp_8wekyb3d8bbwe'; $registryHive = $keyPath.Split('\')[0]; $registryPath = "^""$($registryHive):$($keyPath.Substring($registryHive.Length))"^""; $userSid = (New-Object System.Security.Principal.NTAccount($env:USERNAME)).Translate([Security.Principal.SecurityIdentifier]).Value; $registryPath = $registryPath.Replace('$CURRENT_USER_SID', $userSid); if (Test-Path $registryPath) { Write-Host "^""Skipping, no action needed, registry path "^""$registryPath"^"" already exists."^""; exit 0; }; try { New-Item -Path $registryPath -Force -ErrorAction Stop | Out-Null; Write-Host "^""Successfully created the registry key at path "^""$registryPath"^""."^""; } catch { Write-Error "^""Failed to create the registry key at path "^""$registryPath"^"": $($_.Exception.Message)"^""; Microsoft Learn+2Super User+2 A third‑party tool or script (like the one you used) has changed the policy values. Some of the known policy values under that registry key include: NoActiveProbe (DWORD) — disables active connectivity probe if set to 1. Super User+1 DisablePassivePolling (DWORD) — disables passive traffic‑based detection if set to 1. Microsoft Learn UseGlobalDNS (DWORD) — used in some enterprise/roaming scenarios; not always present. help.dnsfilter.com+1 Possibly others related to “MaxActiveProbes” etc. Microsoft Learn 🔍 Conclusion for your case Yes: having only “(Default)” under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\NetworkConnectivityStatusIndicator is fine and expected if no policy has been set to alter NCSI behaviour. Since you removed the blocking values, everything looks good from the policy‑side. The key still exists (which is okay), but without entries it acts as “no policy override” and the system uses default behaviour for NCSI. Now we just need to ensure: The other NCSI registry path (HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NlaSvc\Parameters\Internet) has the default values (or at least not hindered). Services like NLA/NLS are running, hosts are clean, network stack is good. After a reboot, if Get‑NetConnectionProfile shows IPv4Connectivity : Internet, you’re good. If you like, I can pull a full list of all possible/generic policy values under that key (for enterprise tweaks) from Microsoft docs — so you know what’s normal vs custom. ChatGPT can make mistakes. Check important info. See Cookie Preferences.

body:
JDK. ... Is this Java Development Kit by any chance? 

If yes, it should be something not related to Windows activation. 

What I can suggest is 

1) check the JDK setup file digital signature in properties, double click on the signature, if it says that the digital signature is OK, then the file was not tampered with. 

2) Try to post a screenshot of exact message what type of error you get, and what stage. 

3) The windows you have installed, is it a genuine iso, downloaded from Microsoft directly? Did it come pre installed with the PC? or did you download and install windows from other source?

If you installed Windows from a genuine source and you verified the apps downloaded like JDK exe to be digitally signed, it may be some other error unrelated to the windows activation.

author: ogavick

body:
This is related to Windows installer service. 

What I recommend is the following - the windows installation you have may be  corrupted.

1) in a CMD run with administrator, run: 

sfc /scannow

Wait for the command to complete. Then test again


2) you can check in Services (services.msc)  in Windows, for the windows installer service to be started, if not, manually start the service and try again

3) if none of the above works,  Windows may be corrupted. Try to burn a Windows iso onto a USB, run Windows setup, and in  the setup screen, choose to keep existing files, keep personal files and apps.

This will repair your Windows while keeping your existing files in place. Use it at own risk, even if it should not affect any file on your C: drive, these steps I offered come with no warranty and just as "best effort" solution from me. 

Check here how to make a Windows installation USB and perform an in place upgrade, if the first 2 steps don't fix Windows Installer.

https://www.elevenforum.com/t/repair-install-windows-11-with-an-in-place-upgrade.418/

body:
The other day I used this method to fix a 24H2 install that had been erroring out during each patch Tuesday's cumulative update install for like 5 months. I used the latest ISO for the latest 25H2 and was able to keep everything. I'd already changed it from Home edition to Pro and activated it via this project and all of that was still intact too. I did back up that computer beforehand just in case things went south, but I didn't need it.

Like @thecatontheceiling said, you'll want to free up some space. I'd recommend having 50+ GB free. Using Windows' built-in Disk Cleanup tool in Admin mode and deleting all but the latest restore point on its More Options tab, then checking everything on its Disk Cleanup tab would be a good place to start.

body: if there's not enough space the upgrade should just fail and roll back instead of deleting anything, but regardless you should have backups for your important data
author: ave9858

body:
That 50 GB has nothing to do with your personal files. They are left where they are, which is why it's called an in place upgrade. That space is for a new copy of Windows to be installed, then all of your stuff is "connected" to it, and the old installation of Windows ends up in a Windows.old folder for a short time so you can revert back if you want, or you can remove it manually with Disk Cleanup.

Have you tried Disk Cleanup yet to see how much it actually frees up? None of your personal files or programs are messed with by doing that.

I'm not a fan of sticking my files in the cloud. Flash drives are really cheap (but slow).

**The legacy version of the script will always be available for download.** 

Since all versions older than 10 will no longer receive updates by the end of 2026, the legacy script will not need to be updated either, it will most likely work forever.

author: WitherOrNot

body:
I apologize for the duplicate comment I accidentally posted on January 16 due to a network issue. By the time I noticed it, the issue had already been closed, and I was unable to delete the redundant comment.

As indicated in the [official Microsoft documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/framework/get-started/system-requirements), .NET Framework is not supported on Windows Server 2008 SP2 when installed in Server Core mode, and Windows PowerShell is also unavailable in this configuration. ( Although I have applied the KB968930)

I have tested TSForge on Windows Server 2008 SP2 with the Full (GUI) installation, and it works—though Windows Update fails to detect or install updates automatically. However, I can still apply updates manually without issues.

TSForge cannot be used on Windows Server 2008 SP2 in Server Core mode, precisely because of the lack of .NET Framework support in that environment (I also have tried to install .NET Framework 4.5.2 or 4.6.2 in Server Core mode, but failed). As Microsoft explicitly states:

    “On Windows Server 2008 SP2, .NET Framework isn't supported in the Server Core role.”

This limitation prevents TSForge—which depends on .NET—from running in Server Core installations of this OS version.

I have tried TSForge 1.1.1 in Windows Server 2008 SP2 Server Core installation, it returned 0xc0000135. I have applied ByPassESU-LongHorn-v8 previously, it cannot be removed. Does it might causing TSForge failed to initialize?
author: neville-studio

body:
There are many things required for Windows Activation to work and MAS can identify missing or corrupted required services. May told you this in her reply to you, I quote:
```
Run MAS and see if it can identify anything broken with your install preventing activation
```

I also told this to you in my initial reply:
```
Press 1 when it says "already activated" and it'll tell you if any services need to be enabled or anything like that.
```

If you expect us to list everything that may be affecting Windows Activation, that is not reasonable. The script is more than 10k lines long and a significant amount of it is system integrity checks. Take advantage of the script's advanced troubleshooting capabilities to fix your problem. You can also go into the "Troubleshoot" menu and just try running "Fix Licensing" there, which is very good at fixing activation issues.

I will block you from posting in GitHub issues if you continue acting unreasonably.

body: For those who care, I have solved the problem. The site was blocked by my modern Asus router's security software provided by TrendMicro and not any particular DNS. TrendMicro gives a false positive for https://get.activated.win. If I turn off the software in my router, the powershell script works. But there is no whitelist capability in the program. So I installed on my PC Cloudflare WARP; it bypasses my router's security and replaces it with Cloudflare's security. Cloudflare does not recognize https://get.activated.win as a dangerous site.

body: What was the issue?
author: thecatontheceiling

body:
I was able to resolve the issue, but not by changing anything in MAS.

My Windows 11 Pro had been activated successfully with the same setup for over a year. A few days ago, two things changed around the same time:

- Microsoft pushed new Windows updates
- I enabled a large number of built-in blocking services in AdGuard Home (social networks, streaming, gaming, etc.)

After that, Windows started complaining about activation.

I run 2 instances of AdGuard Home (primary & secondary - Raspberry Pi) - checked their logs and did not see obvious blocking of Microsoft activation endpoints, and general network traffic looked fine. However, after hours of troubleshooting, I tried a “nuclear” test and temporarily disabled/unblocked all AdGuard Home service blocks.

Immediately after doing that, Windows activation returned to normal. Rebooted a few times and no more issue.

So the issue appears to be caused by one or more AdGuard Home service filters indirectly interfering with Windows activation (possibly via shared domains, CDNs, or related endpoints), even though nothing obvious showed up in the logs.

As always, thanks for the project and your time. Much appreciated.
author: DisturbedEagle

body:
**I want to post an update because my earlier conclusion about AdGuard Home turned out to be incomplete.**

Actual root cause and fix:

Before using MAS, I purchased what I believed was a legitimate Windows 11 Pro retail key. It activated successfully at first, but after ~1 month Windows started asking for activation again.

After researching, I realized that key was not legitimate (lesson learned - even with years of IT experience, it happens).

I then switched to MAS and activated Windows 11 Pro successfully. Everything appeared fine. The old (non-legit) product key was still installed in the system. When I reactivated Windows with MAS (manually using pro.xml), I did not explicitly replace the existing key, so Windows kept falling back into a confused activation state. Once I explicitly installed the MAS-provided retail key, the issue stopped completely:

Multiple reboots confirmed stable activation

So in short: this was leftover product-key state, not MAS, and not AdGuard Home.

Apologies for the earlier misattribution, and thanks again for the project and your continued work  - much appreciated.
author: DisturbedEagle

body: The manual activation guide tells you install a generic product key. It was either your AdGuard filters or some other issue in your Windows install. We've seen this issue reported by people who used MAS and didn't manually activate.
author: thecatontheceiling

body:
> The manual activation guide tells you install a generic product key. It was either your AdGuard filters or some other issue in your Windows install. We've seen this issue reported by people who used MAS and didn't manually activate.

As a long-time user and fan of MAS, I initially used the generic key as instructed in their guide (https://massgrave.dev/manual_hwid_activation).

Therefore, I believe Microsoft’s activation servers became confused because I used the “slmgr” command to force my own purchased product key. After running the “slmgr” command once again from an elevated CMD and “generic product key”, the issue was resolved.

As mentioned in my previous comment, this was not an issue with MAS or AdGuard Home.
